perturbation.original_text: All notices, requests, demands or other ------------ communications required by or otherwise with respect to this Agreement shall be in writing and shall be deemed to have been duly given to any party when delivered personally (by courier service or otherwise), when delivered by facsimile and confirmed by return facsimile, or five business days after being mailed by first-class mail, postage prepaid in each case to the applicable addresses set forth below:
perturbation.changed_text: All notices, requests, demands or other ------------ communications required by or otherwise with respect to this Agreement shall be in writing and shall be deemed to have been duly given to any party when delivered personally (by courier service or otherwise), when delivered by facsimile and confirmed by return facsimile, or fifteen business days after being mailed by first-class mail, postage prepaid in each case to the applicable addresses set forth below:
perturbation.explanation: Changing the notice period to 15 days after mailing by first-class mail may conflict with state laws requiring shorter response times for legal or contractual notices.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Texas Rules of Civil Procedure, Rule 21a
perturbation.law_explanation: Texas Rule of Civil Procedure 21a allows for a three-day presumption of receipt after mailing, extending to deadlines. A contractual term of 15 days contradicts the intent for reasonably prompt notification in legal contexts within Texas, as the contract involves parties in Texas and Delaware law defaults to the location where the contract will be performed.
perturbation.justification: The modification extends the notice period to 15 days after mailing, which the Law Explanation states contradicts Texas Rule of Civil Procedure 21a, allowing for a three-day presumption of receipt after mailing. Although there are no scraped law snippets, the LLM's Law Explanation asserts the 15-day period contradicts the intent for reasonably prompt notification in legal contexts within Texas, making the contractual term potentially unenforceable. Given the 'Inconsistencies Legal Contradiction' definition states the contradiction happens when a contract sets a deadline that violates state law, this situation fits the definition.

perturbation.original_text: Seller agrees that during the Non- ------------------ Competition Period Seller will not, and will not permit any of its Subsidiaries to: (i) engage in the Product Business; (ii) encourage any Service Account to specify a party other than CompuCom to provide Products and image load/configurations to such Service Account; or (iii) solicit, entice or induce any employee of CompuCom or any Subsidiary of CompuCom to terminate his or her employment with CompuCom or any Subsidiary of CompuCom or hire any person who was or is at any time from the date of execution of the Asset Purchase Agreement to the end of the Non-Competition Period an employee of CompuCom or any Subsidiary of CompuCom.
perturbation.changed_text: Seller agrees that during the Non- ------------------ Competition Period Seller will not, and will not permit any of its Subsidiaries to: (i) engage in the Product Business; (ii) encourage any Service Account to specify a party other than CompuCom to provide Products and image load/configurations to such Service Account; or (iii) solicit, entice or induce any employee of CompuCom or any Subsidiary of CompuCom to terminate his or her employment with CompuCom or any Subsidiary of CompuCom or hire any person who was or is at any time from the date of execution of the Asset Purchase Agreement to one year from the date of execution an employee of CompuCom or any Subsidiary of CompuCom.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the restricted period for hiring former employees could violate state laws regarding non-compete agreements and restrictions on employee mobility, depending on the specific state's regulations on the duration and scope of such restrictions.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Business and Professions Code Section 16600
perturbation.law_explanation: California Business and Professions Code Section 16600 generally prohibits non-compete agreements. Restricting the hiring of former employees for one year, even without an explicit non-compete, could be construed as an unlawful restraint of trade under California law, as it limits employee mobility.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, BPC 16600 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Business and Professions Code - BPC DIVISION 7. GENERAL BUSINESS REGULATIONS [16000 - 18107] ( Division 7 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 61. ) PART 2. PRESERVATION AND REGULATION OF COMPETITION [16600 - 17365] ( Part 2 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) CHAPTER 1. Contracts in Restraint of Trade [16600 - 16607] ( Chapter 1 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) 16600. (a) Except as provided in this chapter, every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void. (b) (1) This section shall be read broadly, in accordance with Edwards v. Arthur Andersen LLP (2008) 44 Cal.4th 937, to void the application of any noncompete agreement in an employment context, or any noncompete clause in an employment contract, no matter how narrowly tailored, that does not satisfy an exception in this chapter. (2) This subdivision does not constitute a change in, but is declaratory of, existing law. (c) This section shall not be limited to contracts where the person being restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business is a party to the contract. (Amended by Stats. 2023, Ch. 828, Sec. 1. (AB 1076) Effective January 1, 2024.)
perturbation.justification: The modified text reduces the timeframe during which the seller is prohibited from hiring former employees of CompuCom, from 'the end of the Non-Competition Period' to 'one year from the date of execution.' The Law Explanation and Perturbation Explanation both suggest this could violate state laws, particularly California Business and Professions Code Section 16600, which generally prohibits non-compete agreements and restrictions on employee mobility. The scraped law snippet explicitly states in BPC 16600(a): 'Except as provided in this chapter, every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void.' Even though this isn't a direct non-compete, restricting hiring for even one year could be viewed as an unlawful restraint of trade under California law by limiting employee mobility, therefore the contradiction exists.

perturbation.original_text: CompuCom agrees to notify Seller of CompuCom's rejection of such orders for Product within a mutually agreed upon time that is reasonable under the time constraints required by the customer order or contract.
perturbation.changed_text: CompuCom agrees to notify Seller of CompuCom's rejection of such orders for Product within 30 days.
perturbation.explanation: Setting a fixed 30-day deadline for rejection notification, regardless of customer time constraints, could violate the implied duty of good faith and fair dealing if customer orders require much faster responses.
perturbation.law_explanation: UCC 2-206 (1) suggests that offers must be accepted within a reasonable timeframe. A 30-day period for rejection, regardless of the customer's required response time, could violate the principle of timely acceptance or rejection of offers, creating a situation where the seller is bound by an offer for an unreasonably long period.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 2-206. Offer and Acceptance in Formation of Contract. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 2-206. Offer and Acceptance in Formation of Contract. (1) Unless otherwise unambiguously indicated by the language or circumstances (a) an offer to make a contract shall be construed as inviting acceptance in any manner and by any medium reasonable in the circumstances; (b) an order or other offer to buy goods for prompt or current shipment shall be construed as inviting acceptance either by a prompt promise to ship or by the prompt or current shipment of conforming or non-conforming goods, but such a shipment of non-conforming goods does not constitute an acceptance if the seller seasonably notifies the buyer that the shipment is offered only as an accommodation to the buyer. (2) Where the beginning of a requested performance is a reasonable mode of acceptance an offeror who is not notified of acceptance within a reasonable time may treat the offer as having lapsed before acceptance. Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces a fixed 30-day deadline for rejection, which contradicts the original text's emphasis on a 'mutually agreed upon time that is reasonable under the time constraints required by the customer order.' This directly opposes the principle outlined in UCC 2-206 (1), as detailed in Scraped Law Snippet 1, which states that an offer should be accepted 'in any manner and by any medium reasonable in the circumstances.' By setting a fixed deadline, the changed text disregards the 'circumstances,' potentially creating a situation where the seller is bound for an unreasonably long period, thus creating a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement may not be ------------------------------------ amended, changed, supplemented, waived or otherwise modified or terminated except by an instrument in writing signed by each of the parties hereto.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement may not be ------------------------------------ amended, changed, supplemented, waived or otherwise modified or terminated except by an instrument in writing signed by CompuCom.
perturbation.explanation: Requiring only CompuCom's signature for amendments, changes, waivers, or termination could violate basic contract law principles requiring mutual agreement and consideration for contract modifications.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Restatement (Second) of Contracts § 149, read in conjunction with similar laws requires mutual assent and consideration for contract modifications. Allowing only one party (CompuCom) to unilaterally modify the contract violates the principle that contract changes require agreement from all parties involved.

perturbation.justification: The original contract requires all parties to sign an amendment for it to be valid. The changed text only requires CompuCom to sign the amendment to the contract, which removes the original requirement of all parties agreeing to the change. According to the Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation, contract law requires mutual agreement and consideration for contract modifications, which is violated by the changed text. The scraped snippets do not contain information about this law.
